42 macro so today's key macro question
is is the disinflation process still
intact our answers are the December CPI
data were mixed regarding our sticky
inflation theme the core categories of
headline CPI generally accelerated by
the core categories of core CPI
generally decelerated these data and
other leading indicators lead us to
conclude that the disinflationary
process is still intact but barely for
our grid model projections for headline
TPI and our industry leading empirical
Deep dive on us business cycle Dynamics
US inflation is unlikely to return
durably to Trend in the absence of a
recession which implies the highest
probability outcome is inflation firming
over the medium term against easing base
effects late Q2 early Q3 is when
inflation is likely to accelerate
appreciably enough to cause in serious
problems in asset markets coincidentally
late Q2 and early Q3 is when we
anticipate a rebuilding treasury general
account and a hwk a shift in treasury
net financing those Dynamics are likely
to render Global liquidity insufficient
to absorb the roughly record peak in
global debt refinancing demand thus
catalyzing a global refinancing air
pocket if a stock market crash occurs
this summer or sooner we expect the dip
to be bought because investors will
finally find attractive valuations to
bet on the AI super cycle amid tax cuts
and deregulation race yourselves for
while

dashboard as always we wrap up with a
question from our community this one's
regarding term Premia says hey there all
is it possible or likely that the term
Premia beinging below the historic
average could return to a more normal uh
SL exected value by short rate falling
and the long end staying fairly stable
from here rather than long rates
increasing relatively I ask as I try to
reassess I try to assess and
reinvestment versus duration risk at
this point so uh let me start by
defining uh term premum so this question
is is actually regarding the term spread
which is the spread you get uh in longer
Run Dat um longer run Securities as
opposed to short-dated Securities the
term Premia is the excess yield you get
uh for locking up for taking a liquidity
risk on various points in the curve as
opposed to rolling over uh um you know
shorter term uh instruments so those are
two separate things so make sure we have
our uh um vocabulary correct term spread
is the yo curve term Premia is the
actual excess return you get for taking
a liquidity risk in the bond market so
uh going back to answering this question
which is again about the term spread
could the question is essentially asking
could the term spread normalize to a
normal level by having short rates fall
and the answer is obviously yes now what
would cause that based on the current
fed's reaction function is inflation or
sorry is is is is the labor market
slowing faster than what they currently
anticipate um you know they don't really
want to see as pal indicated most
recently in December they don't really
want to see the labor market get
significantly looser from here and so if
it did get significantly Lo from here
then they're obviously going to chase
that with um you know falling interest
rates on on on the short end of the
curve so um now is the labor market
going to get significant loose from here
we would argue probably not um if you
look at certain leading indicators uh
VIs corporate profit cycle or Vis A some
of the um Ser soft survey data you know
we're moving in the wrong direction if
you're expecting the labor market to
fall off a cliff over the medium term in
the context of those two uh Dynamics
corpor profits are accelerating and you
also have uh uh you know you also have
um the uh the soft survey data are
accelerating actually accelerated
marketly as evidenced by yesterday's uh
nfib small business optimism survey so
uh it's very unlikely in our opinion
that the labor market suddenly falls off
a cliff anytime soon which ultimately
suggest that the FED is probably not
going to be able to lower the policy
rate much further from than what they'
already have in the context of our
sticky inflation theme likely developing
and becoming consensus let's call it by
